title: Microplastics extraction from oyster tissue
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"75gv3","text":"Microplastics are defined as any plastic particles that are smaller than 5mm. They have been found in many of our household products, such as detergents, toothpaste, and other beauty products such as exfoliants. It has also been found that clothes with synthesized fibers, such as acrylics, nylon, and polyester shed hundreds of thousands of microplastic particles every time you wear or wash them. Due to the size of microplastics, they easily pass through many water filtration systems and end up in bodies of water, such as the San Francisco Bay or the Pacific Ocean. This protocol describes the procedure to extract and quantify microplastics in oyster tissue.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Take samples of the gastrointestinal tract (GIT) /biofilm and gill tissues (fish gills have the same biofilm membrane)
Weigh out 1-2 g of the tissues. Use a glass cover for weighing.
10% KOH, 50 mL, (as a guideline 1:50 ratio of tissue to KOH works well)
Incubate for 10 min with 10 mL acetone
Wash with another 10 mL of acetone
Staining with 1mL of diluted Nile Red
Cut the sample with forceps to increase surface area
Rinse sides of filter with distilled water
Pour the KOH and tissue solution into the Whatman 1 filter. Vacuum.
Set up a filtration system (Whatman 1 filter, flask)
Soak the Whatman 1 filter in distilled water to prevent it from floating
USE VACUUM (or samples will be compromised due to floating)
Carefully take the filter and place it into a glass plate with forceps
Rinse with 10 mL of water (wash the sides of the filter)
creator: Archana Anand
